comment: Learner-facing explanations for grammar rules the sentence engine applies (see GRAMMAR_RULE_IDS in sentence_engine.mjs). Keyed rule -> support language. {lang} is replaced at render time with the localized name of the language being learned. Authored with AI assistance; human-review before un-flagging a support language in capabilities if quality concerns arise.

en.title: The verb changes with the subject
en.body: In {lang}, the verb takes a different form depending on who does the action — like 'I eat' vs. 'he eats', but for every person.

en.title: No 'to be' in the present
en.body: {lang} leaves out the word for 'to be' in present-tense sentences: 'He is a student' becomes literally 'He student'.

en.title: 很 instead of 'is'
en.body: Mandarin links adjectives to the subject with 很 (hěn), not 是 (shì): 他很强 is literally 'he very strong' — never 他是强.

en.title: The adjective follows the noun
en.body: In {lang}, describing words usually come after the thing they describe — 'house big' instead of 'big house'.

en.title: Small words squeeze together
en.body: In French, little words like je, le, la drop their vowel before a vowel sound: je aime → j'aime, la eau → l'eau.

en.title: 'My/his/her' matches the thing owned
en.body: In French, words like mon/ma ('my') match the gender of what is owned, not the owner: sa fille 'his daughter', son père 'her father'.

en.title: Words agree in gender
en.body: In {lang}, every noun has a grammatical gender, and words that describe it change form to match — a feminine noun takes the feminine form.

en.title: The verb goes last
en.body: {lang} puts the verb at the end of the sentence: 'I food eat' instead of 'I eat food'.

en.title: The verb comes first
en.body: {lang} often starts a sentence with the verb: 'Eat I food' instead of 'I eat food'.

en.title: Choosing the word for 'a'
en.body: The little word for 'a' changes with the noun — in English by sound (a book, an apple), in {lang} by the noun's gender or form.

en.title: Counting with counter words
en.body: In Japanese, numbers attach to nouns through a counter plus の: 二つの食べ物 is literally 'two-items of food'.
